papers.title: Hybrid Approach to Protein-Protein Complex Affinity Prediction Based on Language Models and Molecular Dynamics.
papers.abstract: Protein-protein and protein-peptide interactions are fundamental to biological processes, making the accurate prediction of their binding affinity crucial for drug design and mutational analysis. Here, we develop HyBind-NN, a multimodal graph neural network that integrates protein language models (PLMs) with 3D structural and dynamic datasets to predict protein-protein and protein-peptide affinity. First, we demonstrate that combining ESM-2 sequence embeddings with precise 3D Voronoi spatial geometry enables accurate affinity predictions across diverse structural datasets. Next, we show that t
papers.summary: HyBind-NN is a new multimodal graph neural network that combines protein language model embeddings with 3D structural data to predict binding affinity for protein-protein and protein-peptide complexes. The model leverages ESM-2 sequence embeddings and Voronoi spatial geometry to achieve accurate affinity predictions across diverse structural datasets, offering a useful tool for drug design and mutational analysis.

papers.title: Deep learning model based on MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for noninvasive assessment of lymphovascular invasion in rectal cancer patients.
papers.abstract: Accurate preoperative assessment of lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in patients with rectal cancer (RC) is important for guiding postoperative management. This study aimed to develop and validate a deep learning model based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for the preoperative assessment of LVI in RC patients.
papers.summary: This study developed a deep learning model that uses MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters to noninvasively assess lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in rectal cancer patients before surgery. The model aims to improve preoperative planning and postoperative management by providing accurate LVI prediction without invasive biopsy.

papers.title: Adaptive Self-Attention Graph Pooling for Drug-Target Affinity Prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-target affinity (DTA) prediction is a critical step in drug discovery and precision medicine. Although graph neural networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable progress, existing graph pooling methods rely on fixed ratios, failing to adapt to the structural diversity of molecules and proteins, which leads to information loss or redundant feature retention. To address this issue, we propose the Adaptive Self-Attention Graph Pooling (ASAGPooling) mechanism, which introduces a learnable pooling ratio that dynamically adjusts node retention during training. Furthermore, we develop ASAG-DTA, a mu
papers.summary: This paper introduces a new graph neural network method called ASAGPooling that adaptively adjusts how many nodes to keep when processing molecular and protein structures, improving drug-target affinity prediction. The key innovation is a learnable pooling ratio that replaces fixed ratios, reducing information loss and redundant features. This could help accelerate drug discovery by more accurately predicting how well drug candidates bind to their targets.

papers.title: MD-Transformer: Multimodal Integration of ProtBERT Embeddings and Physicochemical Descriptors for Protein-Protein Interface Residue Prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-protein interaction (PPI) interface residues is essential for understanding molecular recognition and supporting structure-guided design. To integrate contextual sequence representations with structure-related physicochemical information, we propose a multimodal framework termed MD-Transformer. The model combines residue-level ProtBERT embeddings with physicochemical descriptors, including B-factor, solvent-accessible surface area (SASA), and hydrophobicity. A hybrid fusion module first aligns heterogeneous features, followed by Transformer encoding and cross-mod
papers.summary: This paper introduces MD-Transformer, a multimodal deep learning model that combines ProtBERT sequence embeddings with physicochemical features (B-factor, SASA, hydrophobicity) to predict protein-protein interaction interface residues. The model uses a hybrid fusion module and Transformer encoding to integrate these heterogeneous data types, achieving improved prediction accuracy over unimodal approaches.

papers.title: PRED-TMSdeep: Prediction of Transmembrane Topology and Signal Peptides Using Deep Learning.
papers.abstract: Accurate annotation of secreted and membrane proteins requires detecting N-terminal secretion signals, locating their cleavage sites, and distinguishing secretion-signal classes, while also predicting full transmembrane topology for both alpha-helical and beta-barrel proteins. Current tools typically address either whole-protein topology with a generic signal-peptide category or signal-peptide type classification without integrated topology annotation, leaving end-to-end labels incomplete when both features must be resolved together. Here, we present PRED-TMSdeep, a deep learning method that j
papers.summary: PRED-TMSdeep is a new deep learning tool that simultaneously predicts both transmembrane topology and signal peptide types for proteins, addressing a gap where existing tools handle these features separately. It can classify alpha-helical and beta-barrel membrane proteins while also detecting N-terminal secretion signals and their cleavage sites. This integrated approach provides more complete end-to-end annotation for secreted and membrane proteins.

papers.title: Integrative Network Toxicology, Machine Learning, Single-Cell Analysis, scTenifoldKnk-Based Virtual Knockout, and Molecular Docking Suggest a Potential Molecular Link Between Aspartame and Rheumatoid Arthritis Involving HLA-DRB1.
papers.abstract: Aspartame is a widely used artificial sweetener, but its possible relationship with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) remains insufficiently understood. This study aimed to explore, rather than prove, potential molecular links between aspartame-related targets and RA-associated gene networks. Three public RA transcriptomic datasets (GSE55235, GSE55457, and GSE77298) from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database were integrated as discovery/training data. Because these datasets included different tissue origins, batch correction was used to reduce dataset-level technical variation, whereas tissue-ori
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to explore a potential molecular link between the artificial sweetener aspartame and rheumatoid arthritis (RA), identifying the HLA-DRB1 gene as a possible connection point. The authors integrated multiple public RA gene expression datasets and applied network toxicology, machine learning, and virtual knockout analyses to predict how aspartame-related targets might interact with RA-associated gene networks. The findings are preliminary and computational, requiring experimental validation to confirm any causal relationship.

papers.title: Training PBertKla on an Integrated Multi-Source Dataset with a Machine-Learning Layer for Lysine Lactylation Site Prediction.
papers.abstract: Lysine lactylation (Kla) is a recently discovered post-translational modification implicated in energy metabolism, cellular reprogramming, and disease progression. Here, we train the existing ProteinBERT-based predictor PBertKla on an integrated multi-source dataset and augment it with a lightweight machine-learning (ML) layer over sequence-derived features to predict Kla sites; on a common blind test set, the resulting model (PBertKla + ML) reaches an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.9126 on the integrated set and is statistically indistinguishable from the
papers.summary: This paper presents an improved version of the PBertKla model for predicting lysine lactylation sites by training on a combined multi-source dataset and adding a lightweight machine-learning layer on top of sequence features. The enhanced model achieves high predictive performance (AUROC 0.9126) on a blind test set, demonstrating the value of integrating diverse training data and simple ML augmentation.

papers.title: Composition Descriptors and Cultivar Transferability in Machine-Learning Models of Ultrasonication-Induced Functional Properties of Rice Flour.
papers.abstract: Flow-cell ultrasonication of gelatinized rice flour slurries alters cultivar-dependent water solubility, viscosity, and retrogradation of pregelatinized rice flour, properties important for plant-based beverages and convenience foods. We tested whether cultivar-level composition descriptors, amylose, protein, and fiber, can represent cultivar-associated variation in ultrasonication responses while separating process-only prediction, within-domain cultivar representation, and unseen-cultivar transfer. Six rice cultivars were processed across nine amplitude-time combinations and two slurry conce
papers.summary: This study investigates how machine learning models can predict the functional properties of ultrasonicated rice flour based on cultivar composition (amylose, protein, fiber). The authors tested whether these composition descriptors can represent cultivar-specific variation and enable prediction for unseen cultivars, which is important for optimizing plant-based food processing.

papers.title: Identification of Markers on the Basis of Transcriptomic Analysis for Molecular Assignment of Medulloblastoma.
papers.abstract: Medulloblastoma is a heterogeneous solid tumor, and its molecular characteristics are the most important prognostic factors for this neoplasm. Unfortunately, the molecular classification of MB-G3 and MB-G-4 medulloblastoma is very complex because of molecular similarity. Therefore, in this work, through unsupervised machine learning-based gene expression profiling, we identified a low molecular profile associated with four molecular groups of medulloblastoma. We performed medulloblastoma expression microarray data mining via the Partek Genomics Suite and Transcriptome Analysis Console (TAC), a
papers.summary: This study uses unsupervised machine learning on gene expression data to identify a low molecular profile that helps distinguish between the MB-G3 and MB-G4 subtypes of medulloblastoma, which are difficult to classify due to their molecular similarity. The findings could improve molecular assignment and prognostic accuracy for this heterogeneous brain tumor.

papers.title: OmiXAI: An ensemble XAI pipeline for interpretable deep learning in omics data.
papers.abstract: Deep learning methods have become methods of choice in the analysis of genomic data. The performance of deep learning models depends on the information available for training. A growing trend in deep learning applications involves leveraging multi-omics data - spanning genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and other domains. When a deep learning model trained on omics data achieves high performance, the important question is to define factors that contribute to model's predictive power. Explainable AI (XAI) methods can be categorized as model-aware and model-agnosti
papers.summary: OmiXAI is a new ensemble pipeline that integrates multiple explainable AI (XAI) methods to interpret deep learning models trained on multi-omics data. It helps researchers identify which biological features drive model predictions, addressing a key challenge in applying deep learning to genomics and other omics fields.

papers.title: Popformer: Learning general signatures of positive selection with a self-supervised transformer.
papers.abstract: Understanding natural selection can help shed light on the genetics underpinning adaptive evolution. The widespread availability of large-scale human genetic variation data has led to the development of data-driven methods for detecting signatures of selection, many of which are based on deep learning. However, these methods often fail to generalize well to the diversity of selection signatures across a broad range of evolutionary scenarios. We propose a novel transformer-based model, Popformer, for learning encodings of general patterns of genetic variation. Popformer includes site-wise and h
papers.summary: Popformer is a new transformer-based deep learning model that learns general patterns of genetic variation to detect signatures of positive selection in human genomes. Unlike previous methods that struggle to generalize across diverse evolutionary scenarios, Popformer uses self-supervised learning to capture site-wise and haplotype-level features, improving detection of adaptive evolution signals.

papers.title: Construction and validation of a phenotypic prediction model for bacterial gentamicin resistance using deep learning with gene sequences.
papers.abstract: The emergence of bacterial resistance to antibiotics poses a significant threat to human health; thus, there is an urgent need for new strategies in understanding the mechanisms of resistance and further fast prediction of it. Deep learning models offer promising solutions through analyzing genetic sequences in the prediction of bacterial resistance patterns. This study develops and validates a transformer-based deep learning model, DNABERT-2-117M, to predict gentamicin resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae directly from whole-genome sequences. Our central methodological advance investigates the
papers.summary: This study developed a transformer-based deep learning model (DNABERT-2-117M) to predict gentamicin resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae directly from whole-genome sequences. The model offers a promising approach for fast prediction of antibiotic resistance patterns, addressing the urgent need for new strategies to combat bacterial resistance.

papers.title: AI-driven multi-omics deciphering of extrachromosomal circular DNA rescues hidden targets in colorectal cancer.
papers.abstract: Extrachromosomal circular DNA (eccDNA) is an emerging tumor biomarker notable for its tumor-specific amplification and contribution to genomic heterogeneity. However, the substantial heterogeneity of eccDNAs poses a significant challenge for characterization. Current identification tools rely predominantly on single data types, overlooking the integrative potential of multi-omics layers and often discarding sparse but biologically meaningful signals.
papers.summary: This study presents an AI-driven multi-omics approach to better characterize extrachromosomal circular DNA (eccDNA) in colorectal cancer, overcoming limitations of single-data-type methods that miss sparse but important signals. By integrating multiple omics layers, the method identifies hidden tumor targets that could serve as biomarkers or therapeutic leads.

papers.title: The effect of community intervention based on deep learning-established early screening system and model construction for stroke.
papers.abstract: Stroke is a common cardiovascular complication. The machine learning (ML) algorithms in stroke is a new and noble approach. The objective of the study is to study the practicability of establishing a community-based stroke risk exploratory feature identification using ML and evaluate the effectiveness of individualized interventions in preventing stroke.
papers.summary: This study explores the use of machine learning to build an early screening system for stroke risk in community settings, and evaluates the effectiveness of personalized interventions based on that system. The findings suggest that ML-based risk identification and tailored interventions can help prevent stroke, though the work is preliminary and focused on a specific community context.

papers.title: AFD-Net: a robust exchange rate forecasting framework integrating frequency-domain decomposition and attention mechanisms with linear modeling.
papers.abstract: Exchange rate forecasting remains a fundamental challenge in financial time series analysis due to the inherent non-linearity, non-stationarity, and complex temporal dynamics of currency markets. This paper introduces AFD-Net (Attention-Enhanced FAN with DLinear), a novel deep learning framework designed to synergistically integrate frequency-domain decomposition, attention mechanisms, and linear time series modeling. The key architectural innovation lies in the sequential pipeline design: the FAN module first transforms non-stationary inputs into stationary residuals, enabling the subsequent
papers.summary: This paper presents AFD-Net, a deep learning framework for exchange rate forecasting that combines frequency-domain decomposition, attention mechanisms, and linear modeling. The model first uses a FAN module to stabilize non-stationary financial time series data, then applies attention and linear layers to improve prediction accuracy. The approach addresses key challenges in currency market forecasting such as non-linearity and complex temporal dynamics.

papers.title: VITAGRAPH: building a knowledge graph for biologically relevant learning tasks.
papers.abstract: The complexity of human biology poses ongoing challenges, driving global interdisciplinary research. Artificial intelligence has become a powerful tool in computational biology, where graph data structures model entities like protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks and gene functional networks. These networks support crucial tasks in network medicine, including gene-disease association prediction, drug repurposing, and polypharmacy side-effect analysis. Reliable machine learning predictions require high-quality data. We present VITAGRAPH, a comprehensive multi-purpose biological knowledge g
papers.summary: VITAGRAPH is a new comprehensive biological knowledge graph that integrates diverse biological data to support machine learning tasks like gene-disease association prediction and drug repurposing. It provides high-quality, structured data for network medicine applications, addressing the need for reliable inputs in AI-driven computational biology.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics reveals MHC class II-mediated neuroinflammation and systemic metabolic dysregulation as transdiagnostic drivers in major brain disorders.
papers.abstract: Psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative disorders, including Alzheimer's disease (AD), attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism spectrum disorder (ASD), bipolar disorder (BIP), major depressive disorder (MDD), and schizophrenia (SCZ), exhibit complex etiologies driven by immune and metabolic dysregulation. While distinct in their clinical onset, these conditions share overlapping molecular vulnerabilities. This study pioneers an integrative multi-omics framework, combining multi-tissue TWAS, cross-disorder pleiotropy analyses, Mendelian Randomization (MR), predict
papers.summary: This study uses an integrative multi-omics approach to identify shared molecular drivers across major brain disorders, including Alzheimer's, ADHD, autism, bipolar disorder, depression, and schizophrenia. The findings highlight MHC class II-mediated neuroinflammation and systemic metabolic dysregulation as common transdiagnostic mechanisms. This work provides a framework for understanding overlapping immune and metabolic vulnerabilities across psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative conditions.

papers.title: Prediction of axillary lymph node metastasis using a transformer model and multi-omics validation in breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Our study developed a multiomics-driven transformer model that combines mammography, MRI, transcriptomic and proteomic data to noninvasively predict axillary lymph node (ALN) metastasis in breast cancer. A total of 2105 patients from 10 institutions were included for model training and validation. The model achieved an AUC of 0.939 in the training cohort (n = 658) and 0.830-0.867 across three independent validation cohorts (n = 282, 971 and 194, respectively), outperforming conventional ultrasound examination. Grad-CAM visualizations highlighted the tumor edges and surrounding tissue, consiste
papers.summary: This study developed a transformer model integrating mammography, MRI, transcriptomic, and proteomic data to noninvasively predict axillary lymph node metastasis in breast cancer, achieving high accuracy across multiple validation cohorts. The model outperformed conventional ultrasound and used Grad-CAM to highlight tumor edges and surrounding tissue, offering a potential noninvasive alternative for clinical staging.

papers.title: Decoding promoter activity from DNA sequence using pre-trained language models.
papers.abstract: Promoter architecture plays a central role in transcriptional regulation, but predicting promoter activity directly from DNA sequence remains challenging. Here, we tested whether transformer-based DNA language models can learn regulatory logic encoded in Drosophila core promoters. We fine-tuned the pretrained DNA language model DNABERT-2 using a synthetic core promoter dataset measured in S2 cells with luciferase reporter assays. The model predicted promoter activity well when biological replicates were split between training and test data (R² ≈ 0.91), and retained meaningful performance when
papers.summary: This study shows that a pre-trained DNA language model (DNABERT-2) can predict promoter activity from DNA sequence in Drosophila S2 cells, achieving high accuracy (R² ≈ 0.91) when training and test data come from the same biological replicates. The model retains meaningful performance even when replicates are separated, suggesting it captures general regulatory logic encoded in core promoter sequences.

papers.title: EnzymeHunter: Achieving fine-grained enzyme function prediction with a hierarchically aware contrastive learning framework.
papers.abstract: Accurate enzyme function annotation is a grand challenge due to the vast number of uncharacterized proteins and the difficulty of distinguishing subtle functions. We introduce EnzymeHunter, a deep-learning framework that achieves fine-grained prediction via a hierarchically aware contrastive learning strategy. By integrating sequence and structural information and using the Enzyme Commission (EC) hierarchy to guide its loss function, our model learns a functionally coherent embedding space where distances reflect precise levels of catalytic similarity. EnzymeHunter significantly outperforms st
papers.summary: EnzymeHunter is a new deep-learning tool that predicts enzyme functions with high precision by using a contrastive learning strategy that respects the hierarchical Enzyme Commission (EC) classification system. It combines protein sequence and structural data to create an embedding space where similar functions cluster together, outperforming existing methods. This approach addresses the challenge of distinguishing between closely related enzyme functions, which is critical for annotating the many uncharacterized proteins.

papers.title: H3BERTa: A CDR-H3-specific language model for antibody repertoire analysis.
papers.abstract: Antibodies are central to immune defense and therapeutic design, yet predicting functional sequences remains challenging. Deep learning models trained on full variable regions often struggle due to sparse experimental data, signal dilution from conserved framework residues, and extreme diversity of hypervariable loops. The heavy-chain complementarity-determining region 3 (CDR-H3) is the most variable segment, shaping antigen specificity and immune diversity. Here, we present H3BERTa, a language model trained solely on CDR-H3 sequences to assess the extent of biological information encoded by t
papers.summary: H3BERTa is a new language model trained exclusively on CDR-H3 sequences, the most variable part of antibodies, to better predict functional antibody sequences. By focusing only on this hypervariable region, the model avoids signal dilution from conserved framework regions and captures key biological information for immune repertoire analysis.

papers.title: Enhancing tumor T cell antigen prediction by integrating deep protein representations.
papers.abstract: Cancer is a major threat to human health, and cancer immunotherapy is considered the most promising treatment option. It offers high effectiveness and precision, with fewer side effects than traditional treatments. Tumor T cell antigens (TTCA) are the proteins or protein fragments that stand on the surface of cancer cells and that are recognized by the immune system. However, physical experimental approaches to infer them might be costly and time-consuming, even though they are important for cancer immunotherapy.
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational method that uses deep protein representations to improve the prediction of tumor T cell antigens, which are crucial targets for cancer immunotherapy. By integrating deep learning-based protein features, the approach aims to identify these antigens more efficiently and cost-effectively than traditional experimental methods.

papers.title: InstaNovo-P: a de novo peptide sequencing model for phosphoproteomics.
papers.abstract: Phosphorylation, a crucial post-translational modification (PTM), plays a central role in cellular signaling and disease mechanisms. Mass spectrometry-based phosphoproteomics is widely used for system-wide characterization of phosphorylation events. However, traditional methods struggle with accurate phosphorylated site localization, complex search spaces, and detecting sequences outside the reference database. Advances in de novo peptide sequencing offer opportunities to address these limitations, but have yet to become integrated and adapted for phosphoproteomics datasets. Here, we present I
papers.summary: InstaNovo-P is a new deep learning model designed specifically for de novo sequencing of phosphorylated peptides from mass spectrometry data. It improves the accuracy of phosphorylation site localization and can identify peptides not present in reference databases, addressing key limitations in phosphoproteomics.

papers.title: A unified vision-language model for precision oncology and biomarker prediction in neuroblastoma.
papers.abstract: Neuroblastoma is a leading cause of childhood cancer mortality, presenting persistent management challenges due to its biological heterogeneity and the limited accessibility of molecular profiling in routine practice. Here we present NEVA (NEuroblastoma Vision-language AI), a multimodal foundation model designed to address these barriers. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on frozen encoders and multiple instance learning, NEVA implements a pathologist-inspired hierarchical workflow with end-to-end optimization. Developed and evaluated in a large multi-institutional cohort of 1,238 patie
papers.summary: Researchers developed NEVA, a multimodal AI model that analyzes both pathology images and clinical text to predict biomarkers and outcomes in neuroblastoma, a childhood cancer. Tested on over 1,200 patients, the model outperforms conventional methods by using an end-to-end hierarchical approach inspired by pathologists. This work could make precision oncology more accessible by reducing reliance on costly molecular profiling.

papers.title: Decoding cancer circulating transcriptomic signatures with language models.
papers.abstract: Current liquid biopsy methods for multi-cancer detection using plasma cell-free RNA (cfRNA, short RNA fragments circulating in blood that can reflect disease states) typically rely on gene annotations, which can overlook signals from unannotated or repetitive genomic regions. We present GeneLLM, a Transformer-based model that directly processes the nucleotide sequences of human-mapped cfRNA reads to identify cancer-indicative signatures. By bypassing gene-level quantification, the model retains signals from transcriptomic dark matter. The model learns latent pseudo-biomarkers (prototype repres
papers.summary: GeneLLM is a new AI model that directly analyzes the nucleotide sequences of cell-free RNA in blood to detect cancer, bypassing the need for gene annotations and capturing signals from previously overlooked genomic regions. This approach improves multi-cancer detection by leveraging transcriptomic 'dark matter' that traditional methods miss.

papers.title: Mind the gap: An embedding guide to safely travel in sequence space.
papers.abstract: We present a hybrid approach combining a protein language model (pLM) with Monte Carlo (MC) sampling for generating enzyme mutants free of mutations deleterious for structural preservation. Given the amino acid sequence of the original enzyme and a set of residues for which the local environment should be conserved, i.e., the catalytic site, our approach generates mutants that differ vastly in the overall sequence while retaining the geometry of the conserved region, thereby representing promising candidates for further experimental screening. Unlike end-to-end deep-learning approaches, whose
papers.summary: This paper introduces a hybrid method that combines a protein language model with Monte Carlo sampling to generate enzyme mutants that preserve the structure of key functional regions (like catalytic sites) while allowing large changes elsewhere in the sequence. This approach offers a more targeted and interpretable alternative to end-to-end deep learning for designing novel enzyme variants with potential applications in biotechnology.

papers.title: LoMuS: Low-Rank Adaptation with Sequence Multi-representation Improves Protein Stability Prediction.
papers.abstract: Protein folding stability is a key determinant for understanding protein dynamics, including molecular function, pathogenicity, and protein engineering. Yet, accurate prediction of protein stability remains challenging due to high variability in available data, particularly when only sequence information is available and structural knowledge is limited or unavailable. In this work, we introduce LoMuS, a multi-representation-based deep learning model that predicts dataset-provided protein stability scores directly from the primary sequence. In the core of the model architecture, a fusion networ
papers.summary: LoMuS is a new deep learning model that predicts protein stability directly from amino acid sequences, even when structural information is unavailable. It uses a multi-representation approach with a fusion network to handle variability in training data, achieving improved accuracy over existing methods.

papers.title: An End-to-End Deep Learning System for Gastrointestinal Bleeding Detection and Quantification in Wireless Capsule Endoscopy.
papers.abstract: Background/Objectives: Gastrointestinal bleeding is a critical finding in wireless capsule endoscopy (WCE), but manual examination of thousands of image frames is labor-intensive, time-consuming, and susceptible to missed lesions. This study aimed to develop and evaluate a comprehensive deep-learning framework for automated bleeding detection, localization, and quantitative assessment in WCE images. Methods: The proposed framework integrates three complementary deep-learning models: (i) a custom two-dimensional convolutional neural network (2D-CNN) for frame-level bleeding classification, (ii)
papers.summary: This paper presents a deep learning system that automatically detects, localizes, and quantifies gastrointestinal bleeding in wireless capsule endoscopy images, combining three neural network models for classification, segmentation, and severity assessment. The framework aims to reduce the manual burden on clinicians and improve detection accuracy by analyzing thousands of video frames efficiently.

papers.title: Machine Learning-Based Prediction of Masaoka-Koga Stage and WHO Histological Risk Group in Thymic Epithelial Tumors Using Biomarker Combinations.
papers.abstract: Background: Thymic epithelial tumors (TETs) are the most common primary neoplasms of the anterior mediastinum and present a dual classification challenge, namely anatomical staging according to the Masaoka-Koga system and histological risk stratification according to the World Health Organization (WHO) classification. Both tasks rely on expert pathological assessment and may be affected by interobserver variability. This study applied supervised machine learning (ML) to quantitative immunohistochemical (IHC) H-score profiles to predict Masaoka-Koga stage and WHO risk group in TETs. Methods: Lo
papers.summary: This study applies supervised machine learning to quantitative immunohistochemical profiles to predict Masaoka-Koga stage and WHO risk group in thymic epithelial tumors, aiming to reduce interobserver variability in pathological assessment. The approach demonstrates potential for objective, biomarker-based classification of these tumors.

papers.title: A Physics-Informed Dual-Branch LSTM Network for UAV Position and Attitude Estimation.
papers.abstract: To mitigate error accumulation and long-term drift in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) position and attitude estimation using purely inertial measurement unit (IMU) data, this paper presents a dual-branch physics-informed long short-term memory (DPI-LSTM) network incorporating shared temporal encoding, a dual-branch structured regression framework, and physical consistency constraints. The model employs a long short-term memory (LSTM)-based temporal encoder to extract temporal features from IMU time-window sequences. Established inertial kinematic relationships are embedded into the dual-branch L
papers.summary: This paper introduces a dual-branch physics-informed LSTM network that improves UAV position and attitude estimation by embedding inertial kinematic relationships into the model, reducing error accumulation and long-term drift from IMU data alone.

papers.title: Machine Learning and Deep Learning Frameworks for Human-Virus Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction: Emerging Architectures, Methods, Benchmarks, and Challenges.
papers.abstract: The outbreak of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), has emerged as one of the most significant global health crises in recent history. Coronaviruses are a diverse group of RNA viruses classified into alpha, beta, gamma, and delta genera, with SARS-CoV-2 belonging to the beta-coronavirus family. The virus exhibits high transmissibility and causes a wide spectrum of clinical manifestations ranging from mild respiratory symptoms to severe complications such as acute respiratory distress syndrome, multi-organ failure, and dea
papers.summary: This review surveys machine learning and deep learning methods for predicting protein-protein interactions between human and viral proteins, with a focus on SARS-CoV-2. It covers emerging architectures, benchmark datasets, and current challenges in the field.

papers.title: L-Proline-Mediated Modulation of Astringency in Black Chokeberry Puree: Molecular Interactions, Process Optimization, and Quality Preservation.
papers.abstract: Aronia melanocarpa puree is rich in anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins, but its pronounced tannin-derived astringency limits product acceptance. This study developed a non-removal astringency-modulation strategy using food-grade L-proline and evaluated its molecular basis, processing window, and quality effects. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analyses suggested that L-proline altered the local microenvironment of procyanidin B2 (PC-B2) through hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic association, and molecular packing rearrangement, without evident disru
papers.summary: This study developed a method to reduce astringency in black chokeberry puree by adding L-proline, which interacts with tannin compounds through hydrogen bonding and molecular rearrangements without removing them. The approach preserves the fruit's beneficial anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins while improving taste, offering a food-grade solution for product acceptance.

papers.title: Machine Learning-Based Prediction of Antimicrobial Resistance in Escherichia coli from MALDI-TOF Mass Spectrometry Data.
papers.abstract: Objectives: To assess the feasibility and reproducibility of predicting antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in Escherichia coli from MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry data using a standardized, open-source machine learning (ML) workflow, we systematically compared four ML algorithms, evaluated the impact of culture conditions, extract storage, and spectral preprocessing on model performance, and validated results through nested cross-validation with statistical significance testing. Methods: A total of 282 clinical E. coli isolates were analyzed. Two MALDI-TOF MS datasets were generated from freshly cult
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that machine learning models can predict antimicrobial resistance in E. coli from MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry data, with performance varying by algorithm and preprocessing. The standardized workflow and validation approach suggest potential for clinical application, though reproducibility depends on culture conditions and storage.

papers.title: A Fast Demodulation Algorithm for Fibre Bragg Grating Based on the TimeMixer-LightGBM Hybrid Learning Framework.
papers.abstract: Fibre Bragg Grating (FBG) demodulation technology is central to structural health monitoring. However, spectral distortion and noise caused by complex environments, along with the challenge of balancing accuracy and real-time performance in existing deep learning algorithms, severely limit its application in large-scale dynamic sensing networks. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a hybrid learning framework named TimeMixer-LightGBM. The framework first employs a pure MLP-based TimeMixer model to efficiently extract multi-scale spectral sequence features from FBG reflection spectra,
papers.summary: This paper presents a hybrid machine learning framework, TimeMixer-LightGBM, to improve the speed and accuracy of Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) demodulation for structural health monitoring. The method uses a TimeMixer model to extract multi-scale features from spectral data, aiming to balance real-time performance and precision in dynamic sensing networks.

papers.title: AbTune: layer-wise selective fine-tuning of protein language models for antibodies.
papers.abstract: Antibodies play central roles in immune defense and are widely used as therapeutic agents. However, the high structural and sequence diversity of antigen-binding loops, combined with limited experimental data and weak co-evolutionary signals, makes it difficult to develop generalizable predictive models. In this work, we investigate test-time fine-tuning strategies to improve protein language model (pLM) performance in low-data settings, with a focus on antibody-related tasks. Systematic evaluations across tasks show that carefully constrained fine-tuning greatly enhances performance while pre
papers.summary: This paper introduces AbTune, a method for selectively fine-tuning protein language models on antibody tasks by constraining which layers are updated during training. The approach improves model performance in low-data settings by focusing on the most relevant parts of the model, addressing challenges posed by the high diversity of antibody sequences and limited experimental data.

papers.title: Advancing bioinformatics with language models: components, applications, and perspectives.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning-based artificial intelligence models that have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing. Typically composed of neural networks with billions of parameters, they are trained on massive unlabeled datasets using self-supervised or semi-supervised learning. Beyond language, LLMs hold immense potential for addressing complex bioinformatics challenges. This review provides a comprehensive overview of transformer-based model applications in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. We discuss criti
papers.summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how large language models (LLMs) are being applied to bioinformatics challenges, including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. It discusses the components of transformer-based models and their potential to address complex biological problems beyond natural language processing.

papers.title: RD-OMICS: An Integrative Multi-Omics Data Inventory in Rare Diseases.
papers.abstract: Rare diseases (RD) impact over 30 million individuals in the United States, yet fewer than 5% of the identified conditions have FDA-approved treatments. Progress in RD research is hindered by small patient cohorts, biological heterogeneity, and the fragmented, inconsistently annotated publicly available omics data, which limits integrative analysis and translational discovery. Here, we present RD-OMICS, a data inventory with integrated and structured RD omics data from Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO), in the form of a knowledge graph. We developed a metadata harmonization pipeline that combines
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: RD-OMICS is a new data inventory that integrates and structures rare disease omics data from public repositories into a knowledge graph, addressing the fragmentation and inconsistent annotation that hinder research. This resource aims to facilitate integrative analysis and translational discovery for rare diseases, which currently have limited treatment options despite affecting millions.

papers.title: Accelerometry-Based Assessment of Overnight Coat Use on Dog Sleep and Activity Patterns: Implications for Farm Dog Welfare.
papers.abstract: New Zealand working farm dogs can struggle to maintain their body weight and condition during periods of high workload, despite substantial energy intake, highlighting the need to optimise energy balance. One potential strategy for reducing overall energy demands is to reduce energy expenditure for thermoregulation, particularly during colder conditions, which may improve both recovery and energy utilisation. This study investigated whether wearing coats influenced the sleep behaviour, activity, and apparent nutrient digestibility of outdoor-kennelled dogs. Eight adult working-breed dogs (n =
papers.journal: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
papers.summary: This study tested whether having outdoor-kennelled farm dogs wear coats at night affects their sleep, activity, or digestion, finding no significant changes. The results suggest that coat use does not alter these measures, but further research is needed to see if it helps with energy balance in working dogs.

papers.title: A Multi-Engine Consensus Docking Pipeline for RNA Aptamer Screening Against ACC Oxidase (ACO): Statistical Validation, Machine Learning Analysis, and Pilot Cross-Target Evaluation Against ACC Synthase (ACS).
papers.abstract: Postharvest losses in climacteric fruits are largely driven by ethylene, and inhibiting its biosynthesis is an active research goal. RNA aptamers are attractive candidates for modulating the two rate-limiting enzymes-ACC oxidase (ACO) and ACC synthase (ACS), without the toxicity concerns of chemical inhibitors. We built a computational pipeline using three independent docking engines: ITD DOCK (Graphics Processing Unit [GPU]-accelerated, Assisted Model Building with Energy Refinement [AMBER] force fields), HDOCK Lite (knowledge-based geometric potentials), and HADDOCK3 (semi-flexible refinemen
papers.summary: This study develops a computational pipeline combining three docking engines to screen RNA aptamers against ACC oxidase, a key enzyme in fruit ripening. The pipeline shows promise for identifying aptamers that could inhibit ethylene biosynthesis and reduce postharvest losses in climacteric fruits.

papers.title: Affinage: genome-scale mechanistic gene annotation from the published literature.
papers.abstract: Understanding the mechanistic function of a gene is a critical starting point for biology. However, for much of the human proteome that knowledge is scattered across thousands of primary papers or remains poorly established, while the curated databases biologists rely on can lag years behind recent literature. Large language models can now read and synthesize that literature on demand, but doing so faithfully for many genes is an expensive, non-reproducible retrieval session that does not scale across users. Here, we present Affinage, an LLM pipeline that performs this retrieval and mechanisti
papers.summary: Affinage is an LLM-based pipeline that automatically reads and synthesizes the published literature to produce mechanistic gene annotations at genome scale, addressing the lag in curated databases. It retrieves and summarizes functional information for many genes in a reproducible, scalable manner, potentially accelerating biological discovery.

papers.title: Deep learning-driven discovery and mechanism of action study of a minimalist conopeptide targeting α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor.
papers.abstract: Despite extensive structural and functional characterization of the α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, valuable structural insights into its interactions with conopeptides remain limited, thereby hindering the rational development of peptide-based modulators for this clinically important receptor subtype. Here, we present an integrated pipeline combining deep learning, structural biology, computational modeling and electrophysiology to accelerate the discovery and optimization of α7 nAChR-targeting conopeptides. To overcome data scarcity, we developed a deep learning model using the ESM-2 pr
papers.summary: Researchers developed a deep learning pipeline integrating ESM-2, structural biology, and electrophysiology to discover and characterize a minimalist conopeptide that targets the α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, overcoming data scarcity to identify a novel peptide modulator. The study provides structural insights into conopeptide-receptor interactions and demonstrates the potential of AI-driven approaches for peptide-based drug discovery targeting this clinically important receptor.

papers.title: FlowDock: A unified flow-based framework for flexible protein-ligand docking and binding affinity prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand complexes and binding affinity is critical for hit identification and optimization for structure-based drug design. Traditional docking simulates binding processes with searching algorithms guided by energy-scoring functions, which are quite computationally expensive and time-intensive. In contrast, deep learning approaches offer a cost-effective alternative, yet often generate conformations with limited physicochemical validity and fail to account for protein flexibility. To address these pitfalls, we propose FlowDock, a multitask framework enhanced by Ba
papers.summary: FlowDock is a new deep learning framework that simultaneously predicts protein-ligand binding poses and binding affinity, addressing key limitations of traditional docking (slow speed) and prior AI methods (poor physical validity and lack of protein flexibility). By using a flow-based approach, it generates more realistic protein-ligand complexes efficiently, offering a promising tool for faster and more accurate drug discovery.

papers.title: Accurate and task-agnostic modeling of enzymatic reactions through multimodal relational learning.
papers.abstract: Enzymatic reactions play an emerging role in a broad spectrum of scientific and industrial applications. The inherent complexity of enzymes, such as their substrate specificity, conformational flexibility, and the vast diversity of reactions involved, poses substantial challenges for the advanced computational prediction of enzymatic reactions with desirable accuracy. Moreover, existing approaches are mostly tailored for a specific sub-task, such as substrate prediction or binding site annotation, which limits their applicability. In this study, we introduce ERAM, a task-agnostic multimodal le
papers.summary: The paper introduces ERAM, a multimodal relational learning model that can accurately predict various aspects of enzymatic reactions without being limited to a single sub-task like substrate prediction or binding site annotation. By integrating multiple data types, the model overcomes challenges related to enzyme complexity and reaction diversity, offering a versatile tool for computational enzymology.

papers.title: Unveiling the bioactive landscape of drug inactive ingredients (DIGs) using deep transfer learning.
papers.abstract: In a drug product, the major components by mass are the drug inactive ingredients (DIGs), which raises great concerns about their unwanted effects and clinical toxicities. It is demanded to unveil their proteome-wide bioactive landscape using computational methods. However, existing methods are impeded by either incapability to scan human proteome or inaccuracy in DIGs' bioactivity prediction. Here, a cross-attention transformer model, titled TransDIG, leveraging cross-module deep transfer learning was therefore developed to map the bioactive landscape of DIGs using minimal experimental data.
papers.summary: This paper presents TransDIG, a deep learning model that uses cross-attention transformers and transfer learning to predict the proteome-wide bioactivity of drug inactive ingredients (DIGs), addressing the need to assess potential unwanted effects and toxicities of these common excipients. The model overcomes limitations of existing methods by enabling accurate scanning of the entire human proteome with minimal experimental data.

papers.title: Reaction-aware molecular representation learning: Toward generalizable artificial intelligence for enzymatic catalysis.
papers.summary: This paper introduces a reaction-aware molecular representation learning method that improves AI's ability to generalize across enzymatic catalysis tasks. By incorporating reaction information into molecular embeddings, the model achieves better performance in predicting enzyme-substrate interactions and catalytic outcomes.

papers.title: Comparative Evaluation of Large Language Models for Reporting Jaw Lesions on Panoramic Radiographs.
papers.abstract: Background/Objectives: The aim of this study was to assess the diagnostic capabilities of three large language model-based artificial intelligence chatbots (ChatGPT 4.0, Gemini 2.5, and Microsoft Copilot) in the radiographic evaluation of jaw lesions on panoramic images with different densities (mixed, radiolucent, and radiopaque). Methods: 120 panoramic radiographs showing jaw lesions with varying radiographic appearances were independently analyzed using three artificial intelligence chatbot systems. Each model was provided with the same single-round prompt and a standardized diagnostic scor
papers.summary: This study evaluated three AI chatbots (ChatGPT 4.0, Gemini 2.5, and Microsoft Copilot) for their ability to diagnose jaw lesions from panoramic radiographs. The models were tested on 120 images with different lesion densities, using a standardized prompt to compare diagnostic performance.

papers.title: Learning millisecond protein dynamics from what is missing in NMR spectra.
papers.abstract: Many proteins' biological functions rely on interconversions between multiple conformations occurring at micro-to millisecond (µs-ms) timescales. A lack of standardized, large-scale experimental data has hindered obtaining a more predictive understanding of these motions. After curating >100 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) relaxation datasets, we realized an observable for µs-ms dynamics might be hiding in plain sight. Millisecond dynamics can cause NMR signals to broaden beyond detection, leaving some residues not assigned in the chemical shift datasets of ~10,000 proteins deposited in the B
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: The researchers discovered that millisecond-timescale protein dynamics, which are critical for biological function, can be inferred from missing NMR signals in existing datasets. By analyzing over 100 curated NMR relaxation datasets and chemical shift data from ~10,000 proteins, they found that residues whose signals are broadened beyond detection serve as a hidden observable for these motions. This approach leverages vast existing data to study protein dynamics without requiring new experiments.

papers.title: Linguistics and human brain: a perspective of computational neuroscience.
papers.abstract: Elucidating the language-brain relationship requires bridging the methodological gap between linguistics' abstract theoretical frameworks and neuroscience's empirical neural data. As an interdisciplinary cornerstone, computational neuroscience formalizes language's hierarchical and dynamic structures into testable neural representation models through modeling, simulation, and data analysis, enabling computational dialogue between linguistic hypotheses and neural mechanisms. Recent advances in deep learning, particularly large language models (LLMs), have further advanced this inquiry: their hi
papers.summary: This perspective article discusses how computational neuroscience can bridge the gap between abstract linguistic theories and empirical neural data, with recent deep learning advances (especially large language models) offering new tools to model language's hierarchical structures in the brain. The authors highlight how computational modeling enables testable hypotheses about neural representations of language.

papers.title: Predicting ankylosing spondylitis disease activity via patient-reported outcome measures: Building prediction models based on machine learning.
papers.abstract: Disease activity is a critical indicator for monitoring the progression of ankylosing spondylitis (AS), guiding clinical decision-making, and informing treatment plans. Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) have gained prominence in AS clinical management. However, their potential to predict Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score-C-reactive protein (ASDAS-CRP) remains unexplored. This study employs machine learning (ML) techniques to develop prediction models utilizing PROMs data to estimate disease activity in patients with AS.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict disease activity in ankylosing spondylitis patients from patient-reported outcome measures, finding that these self-reported data can effectively estimate clinical disease activity scores. The approach could reduce reliance on blood tests for monitoring disease progression.

papers.title: A cluster analysis of 466 patients demonstrates that glutathione supplementation could preferentially benefit advanced unstable cirrhosis phenotype rather than stable cirrhosis.
papers.abstract: Chronic liver disease is characterized by progressive hepatic glutathione depletion and oxidative stress, yet antioxidant therapies have historically shown disappointing clinical efficacy in unselected populations. This therapeutic paradox may reflect inadequate patient stratification and failure to distinguish between acute reversible oxidative injury and chronic persistent inflammation. We hypothesized that glutathione supplementation may preferentially benefit distinct cirrhosis phenotypes with active systemic inflammation.
papers.summary: This study suggests that glutathione supplementation may be more effective for patients with advanced unstable cirrhosis characterized by active systemic inflammation, rather than those with stable cirrhosis. By analyzing 466 patients, the authors propose that previous clinical failures of antioxidant therapies may be due to lack of patient stratification, and that targeting specific disease phenotypes could improve treatment outcomes.

papers.title: Automated segmentation of pituitary adenomas, pituitary gland, and internal carotid arteries on routine coronal contrast-enhanced T1-weighted MRI: a single-sequence feasibility study.
papers.abstract: Pituitary adenomas (PAs) alter the anatomy of the pituitary gland (PG) and internal carotid arteries (ICAs), requiring accurate segmentation for surgical planning and risk assessment. Multi-sequence magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-based automatic segmentation methods have been employed but remain limited in routine clinical practice due to protocol inconsistencies. This study aimed to evaluate the feasibility and clinical relevance of Swin-Unet and nnU-Net for automated segmentation of the PA, PG, and ICA using only contrast-enhanced T1-weighted (T1CE) coronal MRI.
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that deep learning models (Swin-Unet and nnU-Net) can accurately segment pituitary adenomas, the pituitary gland, and internal carotid arteries using just a single routine MRI sequence (contrast-enhanced T1-weighted coronal images), overcoming the limitations of multi-sequence methods that are often impractical in clinical settings. The approach shows promise for improving surgical planning and risk assessment by making automated segmentation more feasible in routine practice.

papers.title: Historical, taxonomic, and cultural patterns in scientific naming across Animalia.
papers.abstract: Animal naming is fundamental to biological classification and scientific communication. Beyond their taxonomic function, scientific names may also reflect historical and cultural contexts. Owing to this dual role, species names have recently attracted increasing attention from historical and humanities perspectives for both their informative value and the broader patterns they may encode. To examine large-scale patterns in naming practices, we investigated temporal trends of scientific names across Animalia using automated large-language-model-based classification approaches. Our analyses reve
papers.summary: This study uses large language models to analyze naming patterns across all animal species, revealing how scientific names reflect historical and cultural contexts beyond their taxonomic function. The authors demonstrate that automated classification can uncover large-scale trends in how species have been named over time.

papers.title: SLECA: A Single-Cell Atlas of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Enabling Rare-Cell Discovery Using Graph Transformer.
papers.abstract: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex autoimmune disease with multisystem involvement and marked interpatient heterogeneity. This heterogeneity has hindered precise characterization of the immune dysregulatory mechanisms underlying the disease. While rare immune cell populations are increasingly recognized as critical drivers of disease pathogenesis and progression, the lack of sufficiently powered, comprehensive single-cell transcriptomic resources has limited their systematic identification and characterization. To address this gap, we present SLECA, the first large-scale single-ce
papers.summary: SLECA is a large-scale single-cell atlas of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) that enables the discovery of rare immune cell populations using a graph transformer model. This resource addresses the heterogeneity of SLE by providing a comprehensive transcriptomic reference, potentially uncovering novel disease-driving cell types.

papers.title: T-pGNN4DTI: Towards better drug-target interactions prediction using Global Self-attentive Pooled Graph Convolutional Networks and protein pre-training Models.
papers.abstract: Identification of drug-target interactions (DTI) is an important and challenging task in drug discovery and development. Traditional methods generally require biological experiments, which are costly and time-consuming. Machine learning-based methods can rapidly predict DTI using only computer algorithmic models, allowing researchers to validate only the most promising interactions through biochemical experiments. This holds promise for effectively addressing the current challenges of lengthy development cycles and high costs in new drug development. However, it is difficult for the existing D
papers.summary: This paper presents T-pGNN4DTI, a new machine learning method that combines graph neural networks with protein pre-training models to predict drug-target interactions more accurately. The approach uses a global self-attentive pooling mechanism to better capture important features from drug and protein structures, potentially reducing the need for costly biological experiments in drug discovery.

papers.title: PlantFormer: a precise plant disease segmentation network with interactive backbone and global-anisotropic context aggregation.
papers.abstract: Precise plant disease segmentation in real-world agricultural environments presents challenges that general-purpose models often fail to address, primarily due to the anisotropic spread of lesions, blurred biological boundaries, and severe background dominance. To overcome these bottlenecks, this paper proposes PlantFormer, an end-to-end network that effectively integrates and adapts advanced architectural components to address these domain-specific issues. Specifically, PlantFormer employs an InteractSwin Backbone with a Cross-Level Fusion (CLF) module to preserve early pathological details.
papers.summary: PlantFormer is a new deep learning network designed to more accurately segment plant diseases in real-world images, overcoming challenges like irregular lesion shapes and blurred edges. It uses a specialized backbone and fusion module to preserve fine pathological details, outperforming general-purpose models.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new deep learning method that combines a Graph Transformer for drug molecular structures with a CNN-BiLSTM network for protein sequences, linked by a Cross-Attention mechanism to better predict drug-protein interactions. This hybrid approach aims to capture both spatial and contextual features more effectively than existing methods, potentially improving drug discovery and repurposing.
